answer:
Cynewulf’s Andreas approaches human action as something explicitly ordered, enabled, and validated by the divine. In cynewulf-andreas-07b573 chapter 1, the poem opens by saying the apostles’ lots are handed out by the Lord, then shows Matthew praying for help and receiving a direct divine answer, and Andrew being personally commanded to go rescue him (paragraphs p-868413, p-26403d, p-bdbda7, p-4d1e78). Even when Andrew hesitates, the poem frames effective action as obedience to God’s command and as dependent on God’s power; later, Andrew’s prayer and God’s decree produce the flood miracle, after which the Mermedonians conclude that the true Maker sent him and that they must obey (paragraphs p-2e50c1, p-839aab, p-0609a7). In other words, Andreas presents human agency as heroic but fundamentally subordinate to, and empowered by, direct divine intervention.

Bang-E-Dara treats the relationship more inwardly and dialogically. In allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c chapter 3, the speaker urges the human being to look for the Beloved’s trace on every path and frames human action as passionate seeking and prayer (paragraphs p-32c00f, p-0071b9). In allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c chapter 14, man is portrayed as restless, conscious, and burdened by mystery, unlike the rest of creation, which follows its appointed course more simply (paragraphs p-d7ec8e, p-3a62d8, p-3ebd11, p-62d764). In allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8 chapter 20, the speaker says believers fought, preached, and endured hardship for God’s name, but the chapter turns that record of action into a complaint and petition for renewed divine fire and command (paragraphs p-e1af7a, p-a26ec8, p-e3a437, p-82a0c8, p-847833). So where Andreas stresses obedience to explicit divine command and miracle-backed mission, Bang-E-Dara stresses human longing, historical striving, and an appeal for divine response that will reawaken and redirect human action.
